subject: History
overall_learning_objectives: Analyze the dual lineage of the Mughals and why they celebrated their Timurid ancestry
Evaluate the military strategies and expansionist policies of the major Mughal emperors
Understand the structural functioning of the Mansabdari and Jagirdari systems
Explain the 'Zabt' revenue system and the role of Raja Todar Mal
Critically examine Abul Fazl’s 'Akbarnama' and 'Ain-i-Akbari' as historical sources
Analyze Akbar’s transition towards 'Sulh-i-kul' and the concept of universal peace
Discuss the socio-economic disparities and the eventual decentralization of the empire in the 17th century

lesson_plan.title: Origins and the Foundation of Empire
topics_covered: Ancestry: Genghis Khan vs. Timur
The shift from Farghana to the conquest of Delhi (1526)
Comparison of Mughal attitudes towards their Mongol and Timurid roots
teaching_strategy: Comparative analysis: Why the Mughals distanced themselves from the term 'Mongol'
Visualizing the 1526 conquest through military maps
Discussion on the 'Primogeniture' vs. 'Coparcenary' inheritance systems
student_activities: Create a chart comparing the two lines of Mughal descent
Write a short response on why Timur was a source of pride for the Mughals
assessment: Short answer: Name the two major ancestors of the Mughals
Identifying the first capital of the Mughals in India

lesson_plan.title: Imperial Expansion and Military Traditions
topics_covered: Chronology of the 'Big Six' (Babur to Aurangzeb)
Major military campaigns: Chits, Ahoms, and the Deccan
Mughal relations with other Indian rulers (Sisodia Rajputs)
teaching_strategy: Timeline mapping: Key battles and territorial gains
Discussion: The policy of including defeated rulers into the empire rather than humiliating them
Case Study: The challenge of the Marathas and the Deccan campaigns
student_activities: Mark the territories added by Akbar and Aurangzeb on separate blank maps
List the major military challenges faced during Jahangir and Shah Jahan’s reigns
assessment: Matching: Emperors and their significant military achievements
Explaining why the Mughals were successful in expanding their territory

lesson_plan.title: The Mansabdari and Jagirdari Systems
topics_covered: Definition of Mansabdar, Zat, and Sawar
The relationship between rank (Zat) and military responsibility (Sawar)
The revenue collection mechanism through Jagirs
teaching_strategy: Flowchart: Hierarchy of a Mansabdar (Rank -> Salary -> Cavalry)
Distinguishing between 'Jagirdars' and 'Muqtis' (Sultanate vs. Mughal)
Analysis of the increasing pressure on Jagirs in the late 17th century
student_activities: Simulate a Mansabdar's record: Calculate 'Sawar' requirements based on 'Zat' rank
Draft a short diary entry of a Mansabdar stationed far from their Jagir
assessment: Short response: How was a Mansabdar paid for his services?

lesson_plan.title: Revenue and Administration: Zabt and Zamindars
topics_covered: Raja Todar Mal’s 10-year survey (1570-1580)
The Zabt system: Land classification and tax calculation
The role of Zamindars as powerful local intermediaries
teaching_strategy: Data Analysis: How 10-year averages were used to fix revenue
Discussion: Why Zabt was not possible in provinces like Gujarat and Bengal
Analyzing the power dynamic between the Emperor and the local Zamindar
student_activities: Create a table showing the types of land and crops taxed under the Zabt system
Discuss: 'How did Zamindars link the village to the empire?'
assessment: Explaining why revenue was collected primarily in cash

lesson_plan.title: Official Chronicles: The Akbarnama
topics_covered: Abul Fazl’s three-volume work
Structure of the 'Ain-i-Akbari'
The use of statistics and cultural details in Mughal records
teaching_strategy: Reading excerpts: How Abul Fazl describes Akbar’s administration
Critical Thinking: Understanding the purpose of 'Court Histories'
Discussion on the 'Diversity' recorded in the Ain-i-Akbari
student_activities: List the three volumes of the Akbarnama and their contents
Research one specific administrative detail found in the Ain-i-Akbari
assessment: Identifying the author of the Akbarnama
Explaining why the 'Ain-i-Akbari' is a valuable source for historians

lesson_plan.title: Religious Policy and Sulh-i-Kul
topics_covered: The Ibadat Khana at Fatehpur Sikri
Akbar's interactions with Ulama, Brahmanas, Jesuits, and Zoroastrians
The evolution of 'Sulh-i-Kul' (Universal Peace)
teaching_strategy: Inquiry: Why did Akbar move away from orthodox religious views?
Definition: The three pillars of Sulh-i-kul (Honesty, Justice, Peace)
Discussing the influence of Sulh-i-kul on the Taj Mahal and Mughal art
student_activities: Design a conceptual poster illustrating the principles of Sulh-i-kul
Compare Akbar’s religious policy with that of Aurangzeb (briefly)
assessment: True/False: Did Sulh-i-kul promote discrimination against certain religions?

lesson_plan.title: The Empire in the 17th Century and Beyond
topics_covered: The peak of prosperity and the reality of peasant poverty
Nur Jahan’s influence in the court of Jahangir
The fragmentation of the empire into regional states
teaching_strategy: Analyzing the 17th-century wealth gap: Statistics of the Mansabdars
Discussion: How governors of provinces became independent rulers
Chapter Recap: Reviewing key terms (Zat, Sawar, Zabt, Sulh-i-kul)
student_activities: Analyze a pie chart of Mughal expenditure: Nobles vs. Peasants
Complete a summary mind map of the entire Mughal chapter
assessment: Short response: Why did the Mughal authority decline after the 17th century?

teaching_aids: NCERT History Textbook: Our Pasts - II
Map of India showing the expansion of the Mughal Empire
Images of Mughal paintings and Fatehpur Sikri architecture
evaluation_methods: Map identification of major Mughal provinces (Subas)
teacher_tip: Use the 'Zat' and 'Sawar' figures to explain the budget of the empire. When students see that a small number of nobles controlled most of the wealth, the eventual peasant rebellions make much more historical sense.
